deferred: ERR-4b: order-submission path bubble-up — submit_live_order acquires a line on first use of an unsubscribed symbol and surfaces SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT_REACHED through StructuredOrderError
Concrete SubscriptionLineCounter impl backed by ATP_MARKET_DATA_LINE_LIMIT and the live subscription set (owner: SRS-MD-001 / SRS-MD-007)
Concrete RecordValidator implementing the six SyRS SYS-77 rules (a..f) against equity and options records (owner: SRS-DATA-001..006 + SRS-DATA-013)
Quarantine storage backend that persists the rejected payload + reason + timestamp + source (owner: SRS-DATA-014 / SRS-DATA-015)
Notification dispatcher hookup that fans the per-record event into dashboard alert counts and SMTP/SMS reason summaries (owner: SRS-NOTIF-001 / SYS-77 alert clause)

Concrete PacingBudgetValidator that projects watchlist size × per-symbol request granularity against the IB pacing tier (SYS-31's 60-requests-per-10-minute ceiling) for the SYS-22b overnight window and the SYS-23 configured near-close window (owner: SRS-DATA-002 + SRS-DATA-004 + the orchestrator-side scheduler config)
Dashboard alert pane + notification dispatcher fan-out that surfaces SyRS SYS-55's 'until scope or window configuration is reduced' guidance to the operator (owner: SRS-NOTIF-001 / SRS-LOG-001)

Runtime 15-second identical-request throttle (SyRS SYS-31's second pacing clause) — orthogonal to the scheduling-time gate; belongs on the IB historical-data adapter request path, not on the scheduling-time validator (owner: SRS-DATA-002 + SRS-DATA-004 adapter implementation)
Concrete StrategyContainerRuntime backed by the Docker Engine API for the Phase 1 Proxmox VM target (owner: SRS-ARCH-004 + SRS-ORCH-002)

Concrete HealthCheckEventSink wired to the dashboard WebSocket alert pane + the SRS-LOG-001 audit trail + the SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher (owner: SRS-LOG-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 / SyRS SYS-13 dashboard clause)

Container-runtime cooldown / back-off when an unresponsive container restart or an over-deadline destroy fails repeatedly (orthogonal to the gate; belongs on the concrete StrategyContainerRuntime implementation)

Concrete StrategyContainerRuntime that applies ResourceProfile to a real Docker container at create time (owner: SRS-ARCH-004 + SRS-ORCH-002 — same Docker driver entry deferred on the lifecycle contract). Until that lands, the gate validates ResourceProfile as DATA at the orchestrator boundary; no live Docker cgroup limit is applied. Same wired-but-empty shape ERR-2..6 + SRS-ORCH-001 shipped under.

Typed error surface on StrategyContainerRuntime lifecycle methods (create / stop / restart / destroy currently return unit, so a future Docker/cgroup driver cannot signal cgroup-creation failure or insufficient-host-resource refusal back to the launch gate). Owner: SRS-ORCH-001 trait-signature retrofit, paired with the Docker driver — adding the error surface only matters once a real runtime can produce the errors. The contract block already names this as a paired-feature concern.

Production caller of StrategyOrchestrator::launch — the env-var → launch-profile wire is built in this session (profile_for_mode_from_env reads ATP_*_STRATEGY_* via std::env::var, falls back to spec defaults, validates against catalogue bounds), but main.py is still a `pass` stub and no Rust orchestrator service binary invokes launch() yet. Owner: SRS-ARCH-001 production wiring (orchestrator service entry point) — once the binary lands, it calls profile_for_mode_from_env(mode) to populate StrategyLaunchRequest.profile.
Per-strategy override flow from the dashboard / REST API (owner: SRS-API-001 — REST/WebSocket override path layered on top of the env-var lookup once the API surface lands)

workload_event_sink_port.record_signature: fn record(&self, event: WorkloadAdmissionEvent) -> Result<(), WorkloadEventSinkError>

deferred: Concrete HostMemoryProbe backed by sysinfo / procfs / /proc/meminfo (owner: SRS-ARCH-004 + SRS-ORCH-003 — Phase 1 Proxmox VM target). Until that lands, the gate consumes the probe trait through stubs in tests; no real host memory is observed. The contract pin on `forbidden_imports_in_orchestrator_crate` prevents the orchestrator crate itself from depending on sysinfo — the concrete impl will live in a separate adapter crate.

Concrete WorkloadRegistry that lists active strategy containers, ingestion jobs, factor jobs, backtest runs, and Jupyter sessions (owner: SRS-ORCH-001 + SRS-ARCH-004 — same Docker driver entry deferred on the lifecycle contract). Until that lands, the registry is consumed through stubs.

Concrete WorkloadEventSink wired to the dashboard alert pane + SRS-LOG-001 audit trail + SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher. SRS-ORCH-003's acceptance criterion 'dashboard and notification alerts are emitted' is satisfied at the orchestrator boundary today — every refusal / eviction / probe-failure / registry-failure / termination-failure path issues a structured `WorkloadAdmissionEvent` through the typed port, and the 5 audit-event variants (Refused, Terminated, TerminationFailed, HostProbeFailed, RegistryListingFailed) discriminate the failure mode for the dashboard alert lane. Emission is best-effort by design: the gate calls `sink.record(...)` and does not abort or roll back the admission decision on a sink Err (the decision is irreversible once made). The typed `WorkloadEventSinkError` exists so a future caller wrapping the orchestrator can observe sink failures separately from admission outcomes without retrofitting the trait. Durable delivery semantics (bounded queue + retry, persistent journal, fan-out to a secondary channel) belong on the SRS-LOG-001 (audit log) + SRS-NOTIF-001 (notification dispatcher) + SyRS SYS-13 (dashboard) implementations — same deferred dispatcher block that consumes HealthCheckEventSink in the orchestrator-lifecycle contract.

Concrete failure variants on WorkloadTerminationError, HostMemoryProbeError, and WorkloadRegistryError. The gate already consumes all three port methods as Result<..., Error> and refuses safely on any failure (probe error → refuse; active() error → refuse; terminate() error → emit TerminationFailed + skip banking), but the error types today carry only a short reason string. Future variants — sysinfo refresh failed, /proc/meminfo unavailable, Docker engine timeout, cgroup permission denied, registry desynchronized, etc. — will be added when the concrete adapters land so the dashboard can render the specific failure cause.

Workload-agnostic admission envelope. SRS-ORCH-003 ships the SyRS SYS-57 hierarchy as a workload-agnostic data model (WorkloadPriority + WorkloadKind + RegisteredWorkload) but the admission gate currently accepts a StrategyLaunchRequest, which is the right shape for strategy admission (the orchestrator's primary domain — SRS-5.2). Non-strategy admission paths (ingestion-job, factor-pipeline-job, backtest-run, research-session) belong to their owning services and will introduce their own gate methods consuming the same WorkloadPriority library. The structural separation between request envelope and workload-priority is intentional, not drift — the priority parameter is decoupled from request.mode because SYS-57 ranks workloads categorically, not by strategy mode.

Production caller of StrategyOrchestrator::admit_workload — main.py is still a `pass` stub and no Rust orchestrator service binary invokes the gate. Owner: SRS-ARCH-001 production wiring (orchestrator service entry point).

Typed coupling of `admit_workload` to `launch`. Today the two gates are separate methods and `launch`'s Rustdoc states that calling it without first calling `admit_workload` bypasses the SyRS SYS-57 / SYS-58 enforcement. A future session will turn that 'must call' precondition into 'cannot fail to call' via a typestate (`admit_workload` returns an `AdmissionToken`; `launch` consumes the token) or a builder pattern. The typestate change is scoped to land when the production caller of `launch` is wired (same owner as the deferred entry above) so the parameter-list expansion happens once. Until then, the contract-level requirement is exercised by the integration + domain tests for SRS-ORCH-003 (codex critic: arch:admission-gate-bypass).

Gate-level capture and propagation of WorkloadEventSink::record errors. Today emission is best-effort (see WorkloadEventSink trait doc) — the gate does not capture or surface sink failures because the admission decision is irreversible once made. A future session will introduce a richer return shape (e.g. AdmissionOutcome { decision, sink_failures: Vec<...> } or a side-channel collector) once durable-delivery semantics are owned by the concrete SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher and gate-level retry / propagation becomes meaningful (codex critic: adapter:sink-error-discard).
Per-strategy / per-job override of the safety margin from the dashboard / REST API (owner: SRS-API-001 — REST/WebSocket override path layered on top of the env-var lookup once the API surface lands).

Concrete DeployedVersionRegistry implementation backed by a durable store (SQLite, sled, or the SRS-DATA-* state layer once the data layer lands). Today the trait is consumed only by spy implementations in tests; the production caller will land alongside the dashboard / REST API surface (owner: SRS-API-001 / SRS-UI-002).
Dashboard wiring (SyRS SYS-41) that renders the version_identifier on the active-strategy inventory. Owner: SRS-UI-002 + SRS-UI-001.
REST API endpoint (SyRS IF-9) that returns the version_identifier per strategy id. Owner: SRS-API-001.

Backtest result column carrying the strategy code version (SyRS SYS-21). The orchestrator's DeployedVersion record is the source of truth; the backtest pipeline (SRS-BT-001 / SRS-BT-009) will project it onto each backtest result row when those surfaces land.

Concrete failure variants on DeployedVersionRegistryError (durable-store outage / permission denied / mutex poisoning / file-system full). The reason string carries the cause for now; structured variants will be added when the concrete registry adapter lands.

Typed coupling of `launch` to the version_registry record (today emission is best-effort by design — a record failure does not abort the launch). Future durable-delivery semantics on the SRS-LOG-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher path may add gate-level capture of the registry-record Result for retry / propagation.

Source-hash compute path: where the SHA-256 over the strategy source files is calculated. Today the launch envelope receives the hash as a parameter (the caller computes it); the deferred work is the orchestrator helper that hashes the on-disk strategy bundle. Owner: SRS-ORCH-004 follow-up alongside the production orchestrator entry point.

SYS-2e IB paper account integration smoke (gated by ATP_RUN_INTEGRATION=1, owner: SRS-EXE-001 + the future paper-simulation engine). The in-process L7 stub parity test covers the same-strategy-source-runs-both-paths invariant; the real-IB-Gateway round-trip is deferred.

Concrete LiveIBStrategyContext and PaperSimStrategyContext drivers — only stub drivers live in tests/domain/ today; the real drivers land with SRS-EXE-001 and SRS-SIM-*. The parity invariant added here will naturally cover them once they subclass StrategyContext.
Performance-parity surface (SRS-SDK-004 callback latencies: <1000ms p95 live, <100ms p95 paper). Only the shape parity is enforced here; the latency parity rides on the real engines.

strategy_api_scheduler_contract.description: Trading-calendar-aware scheduling primitives for SRS-SDK-002 (SyRS SYS-6 / SYS-50 / SYS-51; StRS SN-1.09 / SN-1.19 / BG-1 / BG-7).
strategy_api_scheduler_contract.calendar_horizon_pin_rationale: exchange_calendars.get_calendar without explicit start/end uses a host-date-rolling default window (~20y backward, ~1y forward). Pinning to a fixed [2000-01-01, 2035-12-31] window makes session-validity decisions date-deterministic across runs so the same historical or scheduled query does not silently flip from valid to CalendarHorizonExceeded as the wall clock advances. Widen the window when SRS-BT-* needs older data or live scheduling exceeds 2035.
deferred: Live wall-clock fire loop is owned by SRS-EXE-001 + SRS-ORCH-* (orchestrator dispatches scheduled callbacks; this module resolves fire times).
Pre-market and after-hours market-data subscription routing is owned by SRS-MD-* (market-data subscription manager).

CBOE Options is aliased to XNYS — exchange_calendars ships no dedicated CBOE Options calendar (XCBF is CBOE Futures with a 16:15 ET close, wrong asset class). NYSE / NASDAQ / CBOE-Options share the SIFMA U.S.-equity-class regular-session holiday calendar in practice for 2026; the alias is correct for the strategy SDK's equity-class option workflows. CBOE-specific option-only early closes (e.g., quad-witching schedule adjustments that diverge from NYSE) are deferred. Owner: future feature that ships maintained CBOE Options session tables. Update _EXCHANGE_HANDLES in python/atp_strategy/calendar.py and re-target the L7 documented-alias test when a real handle lands.

strategy_api_subscriptions_contract.description: Single tradable asset class invariant for SRS-SDK-003 (SyRS SYS-5 single-tradable-class + SyRS SYS-64 structured-error contract; StRS SN-1.07 / BG-1 / BG-5). A strategy configured for equities or options may subscribe to both asset classes for analysis; order submission against the non-configured class raises AssetClassViolation. Identical contract for live IB execution and internal paper simulation per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14.
deferred: MARKET_DATA_STALE and SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT_REACHED structured-error types (per SyRS SYS-64) are owned by SRS-MD-002 (market-data line-limit enforcement) and SRS-MD-004 (stale-data order blocking); the SDK surface here exposes the asset-class guard only.

Live IB and internal paper simulation StrategyContext implementations are owned by SRS-EXE-001 / SRS-SIM-001 — they must call assert_asset_class before routing the order. The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-003 ships a reference _RefStrategyContext that exercises this contract; production drivers re-use the SDK helper rather than reimplementing the comparison.

strategy_api_order_events_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-SDK-004 order event callback delivery (SyRS SYS-7 live-callback delivery + SYS-85 paper engine reuses the same IF-7 API surface + NFR-P4 p95 latency budgets; StRS SN-1.22 / SN-1.29 / BG-1). SCOPE: this contract covers the AC-required *field presence* half of SRS-SDK-004 ONLY. The NFR-P4 *latency proof* half (live <1,000 ms p95 from broker fill acknowledgement; paper <100 ms p95 from simulated fill) requires the real SRS-EXE-001 live dispatcher and SRS-SIM-001 paper dispatcher to exist and is gated on those features. Until then, SRS-SDK-004 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK surface here is the prerequisite production dispatchers will consume. The SDK ships the OrderEvent / OrderEventType surface plus an assert_order_event_payload guard Python dispatchers must call before invoking Strategy.on_order_event so user code can rely on the documented field-presence contract; Rust core runtime dispatchers apply the same field-presence rules locally per the AGENTS.md one-way dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). This metadata block is the cross-language source of truth — the Python SDK constants LIVE_CALLBACK_LATENCY_P95_MS / PAPER_CALLBACK_LATENCY_P95_MS are the Python-side view kept in parity with required_live_callback_latency_p95_ms / required_paper_callback_latency_p95_ms below, and Rust dispatchers read those same keys directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from them). The four AC-named callback categories (FILL, PARTIAL_FILL, CANCELLED, REJECTED) must include fill_price, fill_quantity, and commission per docs/SRS.md row SRS-SDK-004; dispatchers populate explicit zeros on a never-filled cancel or reject and cumulative average / total on a partially-filled cancel or reject. Live and paper modes use the same delivery surface per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14.
deferred: Live IB execution dispatcher (broker fill acknowledgement -> assert_order_event_payload -> Strategy.on_order_event) is owned by SRS-EXE-001. End-to-end live p95 latency proof requires a running IB Gateway and is verified by SRS-EXE-001's contract / performance tests rather than the SDK contract here.

Internal paper simulation dispatcher (simulated fill -> assert_order_event_payload -> Strategy.on_order_event) is owned by SRS-SIM-001. End-to-end paper p95 latency proof requires a running simulation engine and is verified by SRS-SIM-001's contract / performance tests.

The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-004 ships a reference _RefDispatcher exercising both the field-presence guard and a deterministic synthetic in-process latency measurement under the PAPER budget; production dispatchers re-use the SDK helper and budget constants rather than redefining them.

strategy_api_warmup_contract.description: Warm-up mechanism for SRS-SDK-005 (SyRS SYS-8 warm-up + NFR-R3 restart re-execution; StRS SN-1.23 / SC-18 200-bar warm-up / BG-1 / BG-2 / BG-7). The SDK ships a WarmupController + WarmupState lifecycle + assert_warmup_complete guard that every concrete dispatcher (live IB execution per SRS-EXE-001, internal paper simulation per SRS-SIM-001, backtest engine per SRS-BT-001) must drive before delivering the first executable bar. The AC ordering rule (historical bars replayed via Strategy.on_bar before any executable bar, indicator buffers and rolling windows initialised before any trading signal can be generated, on_warmup_complete fired exactly once at the boundary) is enforced at the SDK surface. The controller is fail-closed on a short historical replay: if HistoricalData.get_bars returns fewer than warmup_bars bars for any subscribed symbol the controller raises WarmupNotComplete, leaves WarmupState at IN_PROGRESS, does not invoke on_warmup_complete, and keeps the executable gate (assert_warmup_complete) closed. Identical surface in live and paper modes per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime dispatchers re-implement the same gate locally per AGENTS.md dependency direction (no Rust -> Python SDK dependency).
strategy_api_warmup_contract.required_history_shortfall_behavior: raise WarmupNotComplete (StrategyAPIError); keep WarmupState at IN_PROGRESS; do not call Strategy.on_warmup_complete; keep assert_warmup_complete closed
deferred: Live IB execution dispatcher (live market-data stream gated on WarmupState.COMPLETE) is owned by SRS-EXE-001. The live dispatcher must construct WarmupController, invoke run() before subscribing to live executable bars, and call assert_warmup_complete at the executable boundary.
Internal paper simulation dispatcher (shared live market-data stream gated on WarmupState.COMPLETE) is owned by SRS-SIM-001. Mirror of the live path; the dispatcher must drive the same controller.

Backtest engine warm-up phase (historical replay window split between warm-up and evaluation regions) is owned by SRS-BT-001. Same controller drives the warm-up segment; executable bars begin after WarmupState.COMPLETE.

NFR-R3 restart re-execution of the warm-up mechanism is owned by SRS-EXE-005 (live persistence + restart) and SRS-SIM-004 (paper persistence + restart). On container restart the dispatcher constructs a fresh WarmupController and replays history; the SDK contract here is the same surface both restart paths consume.

The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-005 ships a reference _RefDispatcher and _StubHistoricalData that exercise the AC end-to-end with a 200-bar warm-up + a 200-period SMA. The test proves: (a) all 200 historical bars hit on_bar before the first executable bar; (b) indicator buffers (SMA.is_ready) are True at the executable boundary; (c) order submission attempted during warm-up raises WarmupNotComplete; (d) on_warmup_complete fires exactly once.

strategy_api_indicators_contract.description: Built-in technical indicators for SRS-SDK-006 (SyRS SYS-35 + AC-6 + StRS SN-1.20 + C-9). The SDK ships SMA, EMA, RSI, MACD, BollingerBands, and ATR as thin glue wrappers around BOTH TA-Lib and pandas-ta, choosing the per-indicator canonical reference (see primary_backend_per_indicator: SMA and BollingerBands route through pandas-ta; EMA / RSI / MACD / ATR route through TA-Lib). SyRS AC-6 prohibits custom reimplementations of indicators available in these libraries; the wrappers contain buffering, dtype-conversion, and NaN -> None glue ONLY, with no arithmetic. Each indicator class implements atp_strategy.api.Indicator (value / is_ready / update). MACD additionally exposes MACDValue via .reading (macd, signal, histogram); BollingerBands exposes BollingerValue via .reading (middle, upper, lower). All six classes support incremental updates: a fresh Bar appended to an internal rolling deque triggers a recompute (TA-Lib for EMA/RSI/MACD/ATR; pandas-ta for SMA/BollingerBands) over the buffer and the wrapper exposes the last finite output. The L7 parity test additionally cross-checks every indicator against the OTHER library so AC-6 'wraps both' is verified end-to-end, not just at the import line. The same public surface is used in live IB execution (SRS-EXE-001), internal paper simulation (SRS-SIM-001), backtest (SRS-BT-001), and Jupyter research (SRS-RES-002) per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14. TA-Lib's C library is a mandatory native dependency (see docker/strategy-python.Dockerfile, docker/jupyter.Dockerfile, and .github/workflows/ci.yml install TA-Lib 0.6.4); the Python wrapper requires the C shared library on the loader path.
strategy_api_indicators_contract.primary_backend: mixed: pandas-ta for SMA / BollingerBands; TA-Lib for EMA / RSI / MACD / ATR
strategy_api_indicators_contract.parity_reference: the OTHER library — every indicator pins parity against both backends in the L7 test
strategy_api_indicators_contract.nan_to_none_convention: TA-Lib emits np.nan for warm-up positions where the indicator buffer is not yet full. Wrappers MUST convert the trailing NaN to Python None on .value and .reading so user strategy code does not need to special-case NaN. For compound indicators (MACD, BollingerBands) the whole reading is None if any component leg is NaN.
deferred: Extended indicator coverage (ADX, OBV, Stochastic, Ichimoku, etc.) is intentionally out of scope; SRS-SDK-006 names exactly SMA / EMA / RSI / MACD / BollingerBands / ATR. Future indicator additions follow the same wrap-talib-and-pandas-ta pattern enforced by this contract.
Strategy-author-facing documentation for the indicator surface (usage examples, edge-case guide, performance notes) is owned by SRS-SDK-009 (P1, docs).

Optimising for O(1) per-update cost is out of scope. The SRS-SDK-006 AC says 'support incremental updates', not 'O(1) per update'. O(period) recompute per bar via TA-Lib's C-level loop is acceptable and matches the prior pure-Python implementation's effective cost.

Documented seed-bar divergence between TA-Lib and pandas-ta on RSI / MACD / ATR (different Wilder/EMA seeding conventions) is not a bug. The L7 IndicatorPandasTaParityTest slices the first pandas_ta_seed_skip_bars_factor * period bars before comparing, and the divergence is documented in the indicators.py module docstring.

strategy_api_documentation_contract.description: User-facing documentation surface for SRS-SDK-009 (SyRS NFR-U2, StRS SN-1.01, C-1). The Python Strategy API ships with author-grade docstrings on every public name in atp_strategy.__all__, a README guide under python/atp_strategy/README.md that walks the full SDK surface, and a python/atp_strategy/examples/ subpackage containing self-contained runnable Strategy subclasses that demonstrate every documented capability end-to-end. The acceptance bar (per SRS-SDK-009 AC) is that a Python-proficient trader can author a new strategy using only the docstrings + README + example files, without ever needing to open python/atp_strategy/api.py or any other internal file. The contract block here is the cross-language source of truth for the tier system, README sections, and example modules; the evidence script tools/strategy_api_documentation_check.py and the L3/L7 tests under tests/test_strategy_api_documentation_contract.py and tests/domain/test_api_documentation.py enforce it.
anti_drift_rules: Every identifier referenced by a from atp_strategy import statement in any example module under python/atp_strategy/examples/ MUST appear in atp_strategy.__all__.

Every example module under python/atp_strategy/examples/ MUST import only from atp_strategy (the package facade), from atp_strategy.examples (sibling scaffolding such as the local _harness helper), or the Python standard library. Deep-path imports into platform internals (from atp_strategy.api import _x, from atp_strategy.warmup import _y, from atp_strategy.indicators import _z, from atp_strategy.scheduler import _w, from atp_strategy.calendar import _q) are forbidden because they tell strategy authors to read platform internals.
Every example module MUST define exactly one class that is a strict subclass of atp_strategy.Strategy.

No example module may contain conditional branches discriminating on the strings 'live', 'paper', or 'backtest' (case-insensitive) — SRS-SDK-001 AC-14 requires the same source to run in all three modes.
Every `python -m atp_strategy.examples.<name>` invocation referenced in python/atp_strategy/README.md MUST resolve to a module name in required_example_modules.

Every code fence (``>>> `` doctest or ```python``` fenced block) inside the docstring of any public symbol in atp_strategy.__all__ MUST import only from atp_strategy (the facade) and reference only names in __all__. Deep-path imports in docstring examples teach strategy authors that platform internals are the supported surface, contradicting the SRS-SDK-009 AC.

Every ```python``` code fence inside python/atp_strategy/README.md MUST import only from atp_strategy and reference only names in __all__. The same anti-internals rule applies to the README as to the docstrings.
deferred: Sphinx / mkdocs / readthedocs HTML rendering of the docstring + README surface is out of scope for SRS-SDK-009. The AC is satisfied by the in-source docstring + README + runnable examples surface; auto-rendered HTML can be added later without changing the SDK contract.

Doctest-style ``>>> `` execution via pytest --doctest-modules is intentionally NOT wired in. The author-facing convention chosen here is single-language ```python``` code fences in the README and example-runner verification in the L7 domain test; pytest --doctest-modules would constrain docstring style (mock everything, single-line scenes) without adding signal beyond the L7 example-runner.
Translation / internationalization of docstring content is out of scope; the platform is single-locale per StRS scope.

A formal style guide for future docstring additions (e.g., enforced Google-style or NumPy-style Args/Returns/Raises sections) is intentionally deferred. The contract here pins minimum docstring length per tier, a usable example fence on tier-1 names, and the runnable-example coverage rule — sufficient for the SRS-SDK-009 AC without forcing a particular section header format on the existing surface.

startup_readiness_gate_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for ERR-9 (Missing or invalid startup configuration: hold system in pre-trade state and expose readiness failure through logs, dashboard, and API). SCOPE: this contract covers the *gate state machine* + *structured payload* halves of ERR-9 only. (a) The pre-trade hold state machine that consumes the SRS-ARCH-005 validator output and gates live and paper order submission. (b) The structured payload shape that the downstream log sink (SRS-LOG-001), dashboard (SRS-UI-001), and REST/WebSocket API (SRS-API-001) read when those surfaces land. (c) The four-field operator-override audit-trail contract SRS-MD-006 requires for any manual release of the hold. The DEFERRED halves are: (1) the concrete log sink that persists as_log_records (SRS-LOG-001); (2) the dashboard pane rendering as_dashboard_payload (SRS-UI-001); (3) the live REST/WebSocket endpoint serving as_dashboard_payload (SRS-API-001); (4) the runtime readiness probes for IB connectivity, IB account authentication, SSD data layer access, ingestion freshness within one trading day, system service health, and NAS reachability (SRS-MD-006); (5) the persistent operator-override audit log + notification dispatch (SRS-LOG-001 + SRS-NOTIF-001). Until those land, ERR-9 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK-surface here is the seam those features will plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that need to consult the pre-trade hold state (e.g., the orchestrator gating live order routing per SRS-EXE-001) re-implement the same gate locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). The Python module path and required exports below are the Python-side view; the cross-language gate_state_variants / allowed_transitions / forbidden_transitions / operator_override_required_fields keys are the contract Rust dispatchers consume directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from this block).
deferred: Concrete log sink that consumes as_log_records is owned by SRS-LOG-001. The SDK pins the JSON-line field set (timestamp_ns, severity, key, category, reason, srs_trace) so the persistent log surface can ingest without renegotiation when it lands; the sink itself, retention, and rotation policy belong to SRS-LOG-001.

Dashboard rendering of as_dashboard_payload (operations view + readiness pane) is owned by SRS-UI-001 / UI-1. The SDK pins the document field set (state, ok, errors, warnings, evidence, overrides) so the dashboard backend can render without renegotiation when it lands.

Live REST/WebSocket endpoint serving as_dashboard_payload (e.g., GET /api/v1/system/readiness) is owned by SRS-API-001 / API-2. The endpoint declaration in python/atp_api/routes.py already references readiness as a future capability; the body schema will be the dataclass-serialised dashboard payload defined here.

Runtime readiness probes for IB connectivity, IB account authentication, SSD data layer access, ingestion freshness within one trading day, system service health, and NAS reachability are owned by SRS-MD-006. The SDK contract here is the gate state machine those probes will write into; today the gate is seeded only by the SRS-ARCH-005 static-config half. SRS-MD-006 will extend reevaluate (or add a sibling assert_runtime_ready_or_hold) to fold the runtime-probe outcomes into the same state machine. ERR-9 cannot flip passes:true until both halves are wired.

Persistent operator-override audit log + SRS-NOTIF-001 alert dispatch on override issuance are owned by SRS-LOG-001 + SRS-NOTIF-001. The OperatorOverride dataclass + the four-field audit-trail contract are pinned here; the durable record keeping + the audit-event fan-out are downstream consumers.

Manual-override-mode escalation (e.g., dashboard banner indicating the system is running on an active operator override, time-to-live on overrides) is intentionally not part of the SDK contract. The gate exposes the override audit list so a future SRS-UI-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 follow-up can implement the banner without renegotiation.

operator_workflow_surface_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-API-001 (expose dashboard, CLI, and REST/WebSocket interfaces for operator workflows). SCOPE: this contract is the cross-surface AC-coverage half of SRS-API-001 only. The three per-surface contracts (rest_api, cli, websocket_api) above already pin each surface in isolation; this block adds the SRS-API-001 AC binding that ties REST Capability + CLI Group + (optionally) WebSocket Channel buckets to the eight operator workflows the AC enumerates verbatim: 'Live designation, strategy management, kill switch, Hot-Swap, Reservoir ranking, backtests, system status, and logs are available through documented API paths or CLI commands.' Without this contract a CLI group rename, a REST route deletion, or a Capability/Group bucket renaming would not be caught by any existing test; the per-surface checks verify their own slice but not the SRS-API-001 cross-surface requirement. The DEFERRED halves (concrete request/response handlers, the HTTP server, the WebSocket server, the CLI runner dispatcher, and every downstream subsystem that the handlers will route to: SRS-EXE-001 / SRS-EXE-006 for kill-switch and live-designation IB sequencing, SRS-ORCH-004 / SRS-ORCH-005 for strategy lifecycle, SRS-RESV-002..006 for Hot-Swap and Reservoir ranking, SRS-BT-001 / SRS-BT-009 for backtests, SRS-DATA-002 for watchlist, SRS-LOG-001 for the logs sink, SRS-NOTIF-001 for alerts) are owned by the features named in the deferred[] list below. Until those land, SRS-API-001 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK-surface here is the seam those features will plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 / ERR-9 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that need to consult the documented operator workflow set re-derive the same workflow IDs locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK).
operator_workflow_surface_contract.ac_phrase_canonical: Live designation, strategy management, kill switch, Hot-Swap, Reservoir ranking, backtests, system status, and logs

deferred.what: Real kill-switch + live-designation route handlers + IB cancel / liquidate / halt / disconnect sequence. The SDK pins the route paths, request fields, response fields, and the requires_confirmation guard so the handler can land without renegotiation; the IB-side sequencing belongs to SRS-EXE-001.
Headless IB Gateway brokerage adapter the kill-switch / live-designation handlers route to. Until this lands the kill-switch and live-designation REST routes have no IB-side execution partner.

Strategy lifecycle dispatcher behind /api/v1/strategies and the `strategy` CLI group (start / stop / restart / rollback). The SDK pins the action vocabulary and the deployment_version_hash response field; the orchestrator wiring belongs to SRS-ORCH-004.

Strategy rollback target_version_hash resolution + live-rollback confirmation guard. The SDK pins requires_confirmation on the lifecycle rollback action; the orchestrator-side rollback semantics belong to SRS-ORCH-005.

Hot-Swap trigger handler + demotion-then-promotion sequencing behind /api/v1/hot-swap and the `hot-swap trigger` CLI command. The SDK pins requires_confirmation and the candidate_strategy_id request field; the Reservoir-side sequencing belongs to SRS-RESV-003.

Reservoir ranking query handler behind /api/v1/reservoir/ranking. The SDK pins eval_window_days, rankings, sharpe, sortino, and momentum_score field shapes; the ranking engine belongs to SRS-RESV-002.

Backtest launch handler behind /api/v1/backtests. The SDK pins strategy_id, start_date, end_date, parameter_overrides, cost_model, uploaded_parquet_id request fields and the backtest_id + queued_at response; the backtest runner belongs to SRS-BT-001.

Backtest results query handler behind GET /api/v1/backtests/{backtest_id}. The SDK pins the metrics + trade_log + equity_curve + benchmark_comparison response shape; the persistence layer belongs to SRS-BT-009.
Watchlist GET/PUT handlers behind /api/v1/watchlist. The SDK pins the symbols list and pacing_budget_remaining response field; the IB minute-bar pacing budget belongs to SRS-DATA-002.

Log query handler behind /api/v1/logs + the `admin logs` CLI command + the LOGS WebSocket channel publisher. The SDK pins the log_class / severity / source / event_type / correlation_id / start_time / end_time request fields and the events response shape; the system-log vs strategy-log separation and the persistent sink belong to SRS-LOG-001.

Alert query handler behind /api/v1/alerts + the `admin alerts` CLI command + the ALERTS WebSocket channel publisher. The SDK pins the alert_id / raised_at / severity / channel / delivery_status / acknowledged response shape; the notification dispatch and delivery accounting belong to SRS-NOTIF-001.

Dashboard binding to the WebSocket channels (PNL, METRICS, ACCOUNT_STATUS, HEARTBEAT, STRATEGY_STATE, RESERVOIR_RANKING) and the REST surface. The SDK pins the channel set and refresh ceilings (NFR-P2 ≤5 s); the dashboard rendering belongs to SRS-UI-001.

The HTTP server + WebSocket server + CLI runner that bind the declared routes / channels / commands to the handlers above. The SDK contract here is server-framework-agnostic: a future runtime session picks the framework (e.g. starlette + uvicorn for REST/WS, argparse + python -m atp_cli for CLI) and wires the dispatchers; the contract block names the surface shape so the runtime cannot drift from it.

log_record_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-LOG-001 (separate persistent system logs from user strategy logs; render each entry with timestamp / severity / source / event type / message / correlation ID; both classes viewable from the dashboard). SCOPE: this contract covers the *structured record schema* + *sink-routing boundary* halves of SRS-LOG-001 only. (a) The LogRecord dataclass + Severity / Source / LogClass StrEnums pin the six AC-named fields + the SRS-LOG-001 separation discriminant + the optional strategy_id that the strategy half requires. (b) The RoutedLogDispatcher + LogSink Protocol pin the seam SRS-LOG-001's persistent sinks plug into when they land; the dispatcher validates the schema at the dispatch boundary and routes on log_class so a SYSTEM record cannot land in the strategy sink or vice versa. (c) EVENT_TYPES_BY_SOURCE pins the SyRS SYS-61 / SRS-LOG-001 enumerations of system event types (order routing decisions/outcomes, ingestion start/completion/failure, container start/stop/restart/OOM-kill, IB Gateway connect/disconnect/reconnect, kill switch activations, Hot-Swap promotion/demotion, resource threshold alerts, market data subscription changes). The DEFERRED halves are: (1) the concrete persistent sinks (file / SQLite / Loki / whatever the runtime picks) owned by SRS-LOG-001's runtime half; (2) the dashboard log pane rendering owned by SRS-UI-001; (3) the live GET /api/v1/logs REST handler body owned by SRS-API-001; (4) the LOGS WebSocket channel publisher owned by SRS-API-001; (5) the admin logs CLI runner owned by SRS-API-001's operator-interface-runtime; (6) the audit-log retention + rotation policy owned by SRS-LOG-001. Until those land, SRS-LOG-001 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK-surface here is the seam those features will plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 / ERR-9 / SRS-API-001 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that emit logs re-implement the same record locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). The Python module path and required exports below are the Python-side view; the cross-language severity_variants / log_class_variants / source_variants / system_source_variants / event_types_by_source keys are the contract Rust emitters consume directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from this block).

deferred.what: Concrete persistent sinks for the system + strategy halves (file / SQLite / Loki / whatever the runtime picks), retention + rotation policy, and the queryable index that GET /api/v1/logs reads from. The SDK pins the record schema, the LogSink Protocol, and the system-vs-strategy separation invariant so the runtime sinks can plug in without renegotiation; the persistence + retention belong to SRS-LOG-001's runtime half.

Dashboard log pane rendering for both the system and strategy log classes (per the AC 'both log classes are viewable from the dashboard' half). The SDK pins the LogRecord.as_dict() shape so the dashboard backend can render without renegotiation; the rendering belongs to SRS-UI-001.

Live GET /api/v1/logs REST handler body + LOGS WebSocket channel publisher + admin logs CLI runner. The route / channel / command declarations already exist in python/atp_api/routes.py, python/atp_ws/channels.py, and python/atp_cli/commands.py (pinned by the operator_workflow_surface_contract block); the request/response wiring + the SSE/WS streaming belong to SRS-API-001's operator-interface-runtime.

Notification dispatch when ERROR / CRITICAL records land (IB connectivity loss, kill-switch activation, resource alerts). The SDK pins the severity enum + the source enum so the notification subsystem can subscribe to a filtered stream without renegotiation; the dispatcher fan-out + email/SMS delivery belong to SRS-NOTIF-001.

Strategy-side logging API exposed within the Python Strategy API (e.g., self.log.info(...) inside on_data). The LogRecord schema + LogClass.STRATEGY half + Source.STRATEGY pinning are the seam this future SDK convenience helper plugs into; the helper itself belongs to a follow-up SRS-SDK feature.

The HTTP server + WebSocket server + CLI runner that bind the GET /api/v1/logs route, LOGS WebSocket channel, and admin logs CLI command to the persistent sinks. Same downstream owner as the SRS-API-001 deferral above; named here so the contract block enumerates every consumer of the SDK seam.

subscription_fanout_contract.description: SRS-MD-001 is the consolidation + fan-out half of SyRS SYS-70 (the line-limit half is SRS-MD-002 / subscription_limit_contract). This SDK-surface models the structural contract: ConsolidatedSubscriptionRegistry in atp-market-data owns the live subscription set, keeps exactly ONE upstream IB market-data subscription per distinct security regardless of how many strategy containers subscribe, fans received MarketDataTicks out to every subscriber of the tick symbol (and to no other subscriber), publishes a SubscriptionChangeEvent (SyRS SYS-61 / SRS-LOG-001 subscription_change, Source.MARKET_DATA) on every line-affecting / dedup transition, and serves as the concrete SubscriptionLineCounter the SRS-MD-002 limit gate consumes (closing subscription_limit_contract.deferred[]'s 'owner: SRS-MD-001' item). The consolidated set is keyed on a canonical SecurityKey (normalized symbol + AssetClass) so case/whitespace variants share one line; admission is ATOMIC — subscribe enforces the configured line ceiling in the same mutable borrow that performs the insert (no probe-then-mutate race) and returns SubscriptionRegistryError::LineLimitReached when a new line would exceed the cap. SecurityKey fails closed on AssetClass::Option (SecurityKeyError::OptionContractIdentityRequired -> SubscriptionRegistryError::OptionContractUnsupported): a real option contract needs underlying + expiration + strike + right, which is deferred to SRS-DATA-004 / SRS-EXE-004, so the manager rejects option subscriptions rather than conflating distinct contracts on one underlying. SubscriptionChangeEvent carries asset_class so the line-usage stream stays unambiguous. The check PASS line is 'SRS-MD-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps passes:false because the AC's runtime halves (real IB feed, async fan-out transport, the <=100 ms latency NFR) are deferred (see deferred[]).

deferred.what: The real IB upstream binding (reqMktData / cancelMktData driven by the registry's Opened / Closed transitions), live tick ingestion, the async fan-out transport that delivers MarketDataTicks to subscriber containers, and thread-safe concurrency. ConsolidatedSubscriptionRegistry is single-threaded and models the structural dedup + fan-out + line-accounting only; the runtime that wraps it flips SRS-MD-001 passes:true together with SRS-PERF-001.

The <=100 ms fan-out latency acceptance criterion measured against a PTP-disciplined host clock with p50/p95/p99/p99.9 reporting. The structural fan-out contract lives here; the latency measurement harness is the deferred performance half (same pattern as SRS-SDK-004's deferred p95 callback latency).

The canonical option contract identity (underlying + expiration + strike + call/put right, or a normalized vendor-neutral contract id) that AssetClass::Option requires. SecurityKey::new fails closed on options (SecurityKeyError::OptionContractIdentityRequired) so distinct contracts on one underlying never silently consolidate; AssetClass::Option + the asset_class field are the forward-compatible seam those features extend. Raised by the /codex:adversarial-review round-2 [high] finding; deferred per the user's scope decision rather than modeling option contracts ahead of the features that own them.

The headless IB Gateway brokerage adapter that owns the actual reqMktData / cancelMktData API calls the registry's Opened (first subscriber) and Closed (last subscriber) transitions must drive. The adapter is isolated behind the broker interface per SRS-ARCH-003; the registry never names a vendor token.

Heartbeat freshness monitoring (SRS-MD-003) and tick sequence-gap detection (SRS-MD-007) that mark a consolidated subscription stale. MarketDataTick.tick_seq is an opaque per-delivery counter for fan-out correlation only; sequence-GAP semantics are deliberately MD-007's concern, not modeled here.

The persistent system-log sink for the subscription_change events (Source.MARKET_DATA, event_type subscription_change pinned in log_record_contract). SubscriptionChangeEvent is shaped to feed the LogRecord seam; the concrete sink + routing is owned by the SRS-LOG-001 runtime.

The dashboard subscription / line-usage pane and the REST/WebSocket surfacing of lines_in_use, subscriber_count, and consolidated-subscription operator alerts. The registry exposes the read accessors (distinct_subscriptions, subscriber_count); binding them to the dashboard/API is the deferred operator-interface half.

Distinguishing an UNSUPPORTED / uncanonicalizable security identity from genuine line-capacity exhaustion at the SRS-MD-002 gate. try_acquire fails closed by returning ExceededLimit for an option / empty-symbol request, so the gate REJECTS it correctly (no false acceptance) but labels it SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT_REACHED and emits a SubscriptionLimitEvent even below capacity. A precise unsupported/invalid-identity error requires either widening SubscriptionLineCounter::try_acquire to a typed result or adding a pre-probe validation stage to MarketDataSubscriptionManager::request_subscription (both MD-002 gate surface) — and lands naturally with the option-support features (which make options representable) plus the operator-interface runtime (which surfaces alerts). Deferred per the user's scope decision: raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-4 [medium]; the request is already rejected fail-closed (safe), and the runtime that would surface a misleading alert does not exist yet (SRS-MD-001 stays passes:false).

deferred: Concrete HotSwapLiquidationProbe driving the real SRS-RESV-004 demotion sequence (stop new signals, cancel resting IB orders, submit liquidation orders) and the 60-second async wait loop awaiting flat confirmation (owner: SRS-RESV-003 / SRS-RESV-004 / SRS-RESV-006 Hot-Swap runtime) — the slice models only the binary outcome the loop produces.

Full probe outcome-consistency hardening: the gate normalises a FlatBeforeTimeout-over-deadline into a timeout (fail closed toward blocking promotion — always safe), but it does NOT defend the inverse — a TimedOutDemotionPending reported before request.timeout_seconds elapses (or with a mismatched timeout_seconds) would fire the destructive unfilled-order cancel early and return a misleading 'liquidation timeout'. Handling that correctly requires a distinct probe-inconsistency rejection that blocks promotion WITHOUT the premature cancel (block-without-cancel vs block-with-cancel), which is the deferred Hot-Swap runtime's richer demotion semantics. Until then the probe's outcome-consistency (flat ⟹ within deadline; timeout ⟹ at/after the deadline with a matching timeout_seconds) is the HotSwapLiquidationProbe contract precondition; the shipped slice has no concrete probe so none can violate it. Raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-4 [high]; deferred per the user's scope decision (owner: SRS-RESV-003 / SRS-RESV-004 / SRS-RESV-006 Hot-Swap runtime probe).

Durable demotion-pending lockout + manual-resolution clearing: resolve_demotion is a STATELESS single-attempt decision surface — on timeout it cancels, alerts, records, fails closed, and blocks promotion for THAT call, but it does not persist a demotion-pending lock keyed by the attempt, so SRS-RESV-004's 'promotion is blocked until manual resolution' is not enforced across a later retry whose probe reports flat. The persistent demotion-pending store/registry (written before the timeout error, consulted before accepting any later flat result) plus the operator manual-resolution command that clears it are the deferred Hot-Swap runtime (owner: SRS-RESV-003 / SRS-RESV-004 / SRS-RESV-006 state machine + the SRS-API-001 manual-resolution operator workflow) — the same promote/demote/rollback subsystem tracked by the skipped tests/domain/test_single_live_invariant.py. Raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-3 [high]; deferred per the user's scope decision (building the stateful lockout here would expand the SDK-surface slice into the multi-session Hot-Swap runtime; ERR-7 stays passes:false).

Concrete UnfilledOrderCanceller routing the timeout-branch cancel to the IB adapter's BrokerageAdapter::cancel_order (owner: SRS-EXE-006 + the atp-adapters IB integration). The port is fallible (returns Result<(), HotSwapSideEffectError>) and resolve_demotion records the outcome on HotSwapDemotionEvent.liquidation_cancel; the typed CONNECTIVITY_BLOCKED / STALE_DATA_BLOCKED / transport-timeout failure taxonomy (vs today's reason-string HotSwapSideEffectError) lands with the IB-cancel runtime.

Concrete OperatorAlertSink email/SMS transport — atp-notification is a stub today; the dashboard/email/SMS dispatcher is the deferred SRS-NOTIF-001 fan-out. The port is fallible (returns Result<(), HotSwapSideEffectError>) and resolve_demotion records the outcome on HotSwapDemotionEvent.operator_alert; the typed transport-failure taxonomy lands with the SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher.
SRS-LOG-001 persistent sink consuming HotSwapDemotionEvent (the Hot-Swap demotion/promotion log event class) and SRS-UI-001 dashboard demotion-pending pane (UI-5 'inspect demotion-pending state').

SRS-API-001 /api/v1/hot-swap REST handler + 'hot-swap' CLI body that drive resolve_demotion and surface the demotion-pending / promotion-blocked state to the operator (owner: SRS-RESV-003 live sequencing).

Concrete KillSwitchLiquidationProbe driving the real SRS-SAFE-001 QuantConnect Liquidate sequence (cancel all resting IB orders, submit market liquidation orders for every open live-strategy position, halt the paper simulation engines, disconnect from IB) and the 30-second async wait loop awaiting fill confirmation (owner: SRS-SAFE-001 kill-switch runtime + NFR-P3 5-second activation budget) — the slice models only the binary SRS-SAFE-002 timeout outcome the loop produces.

Typed KillSwitchProbeError taxonomy: the fill-confirmation probe returns Result<KillSwitchLiquidationOutcome, KillSwitchProbeError> so connectivity loss / order-state unavailability / probe timeout surface as a typed error (the gate fails closed via StructuredKillSwitchTimeoutError::probe_unavailable — distinct category KILL_SWITCH_LIQUIDATION_PROBE_UNAVAILABLE — taking NO automated cancel/disconnect on the unconfirmable order state). Today KillSwitchProbeError carries a reason string; the structured CONNECTIVITY_BLOCKED / order-state / transport-timeout variants land with the concrete probe runtime (owner: SRS-EXE-006 IB adapter). The operator notification + order resolution on the unconfirmable state are the caller's / deferred kill-switch runtime's responsibility.

Full probe outcome-consistency hardening: the gate normalises a FilledBeforeTimeout-over-deadline into a timeout (fail closed toward running the SYS-44b cleanup — the safe direction), but it does NOT defend the inverse — a TimedOutUnfilled reported before request.timeout_seconds elapses (or with a mismatched timeout_seconds) would fire the destructive cancel + disconnect early. Handling that correctly requires a distinct probe-inconsistency rejection without the premature cancel/disconnect, which is the deferred kill-switch runtime's richer semantics. Until then the probe's outcome-consistency (filled ⟹ within deadline; timeout ⟹ at/after the deadline with a matching timeout_seconds) is the KillSwitchLiquidationProbe contract precondition; the shipped slice has no concrete probe so none can violate it.

Durable post-timeout lockout: resolve_kill_switch_timeout is a STATELESS single-attempt decision surface — on timeout it pages, cancels, disconnects, records, and refuses for THAT call, but it does not persist a kill-switch lockout, so SYS-44b's 'the operator shall resolve remaining positions manually' is not enforced across a later retry. The persistent post-timeout store + operator manual-resolution command are the deferred kill-switch runtime (owner: SRS-SAFE-001 + the SRS-API-001 manual-resolution operator workflow).

Concrete IbLiquidationCleanup routing the timeout-branch cancel + disconnect to the IB adapter's BrokerageAdapter::cancel_order and disconnect (owner: SRS-EXE-006 + the atp-adapters IB integration). The port is fallible (returns Result<(), KillSwitchSideEffectError>) and resolve_kill_switch_timeout records each outcome on KillSwitchTimeoutEvent.liquidation_cancel / .ib_disconnect; the typed CONNECTIVITY_BLOCKED / transport-timeout failure taxonomy (vs today's reason-string KillSwitchSideEffectError) lands with the IB runtime.

Concrete KillSwitchOperatorAlertSink email/SMS transport — atp-notification is a stub today; the email/SMS dispatcher is the deferred SRS-NOTIF-001 fan-out. The port is fallible (returns Result<(), KillSwitchSideEffectError>) and resolve_kill_switch_timeout records the outcome on KillSwitchTimeoutEvent.operator_alert; the typed transport-failure taxonomy lands with the SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher.

SRS-LOG-001 persistent sink consuming KillSwitchTimeoutEvent (the kill-switch-activation log event class, SYS-61) and SRS-UI-001 dashboard kill-switch pane (UI-4 'see cancellation, liquidation submission, timeout, notification, and disconnect status').

SRS-SAFE-002 fault-injection scenario + performance test driving resolve_kill_switch_timeout through the live kill-switch + IB-adapter runtime; ERR-8 stays passes:false until that end-to-end path exists.

backtest_contract.description: SRS-BT-001 (backtest Python strategies against stored data and user-uploaded Parquet data over configurable date ranges). This SDK-surface ships a GENUINELY RUNNABLE deterministic backtest engine in crates/atp-simulation (module `backtest`): BacktestEngine::run takes a BacktestRequest (a configurable inclusive DateRange, a BacktestDataSource = SystemData | UploadedData, and a starting cash balance), a BarSource, and a BacktestStrategy; it restricts replay to the configurable window, replays bars in deterministic timestamp order, drives the strategy bar-by-bar, applies deterministic fills, and produces a real trade log (Vec<Fill>) + equity curve (Vec<EquityPoint>) + final equity. All money math is in integer minor units (i64) with i128 intermediates and checked_add / checked_sub / try_from arithmetic (the free fn checked_notional) — never f64 — so it is exact and overflow-safe (BacktestError::Overflow). The engine fails closed on an empty symbol (EmptySymbol), an inverted window (InvalidDateRange), and a window with no bars (EmptyData). Because the BarSource (uploaded Parquet / system catalog) is a trust boundary, the engine validates provenance — BarSource::source() must equal request.data_source or the run fails closed (DataSourceMismatch), so a result can never misreport which dataset it ran against — and also rejects a source that returns a bar for a different symbol (UnexpectedSymbol — never silently trade foreign prices), duplicate bars for one symbol+timestamp (DuplicateBar — a duplicate market record would double-fill one instant and make replay order-dependent), and any replayed bar with a non-positive close price (NonPositivePrice — a negative close would otherwise flip a buy's checked_sub into a cash-fabricating addition) before any fill is applied. The strategy port BacktestStrategy::on_bar is fallible (returns Result<i64, BacktestError>): because the deferred Python host can raise / time out / fail to marshal, a strategy failure surfaces as StrategyFailed and the engine aborts before applying any fill, never silently treating a failure as a 0 delta. The engine also fails closed (TooManyBars) when the IN-WINDOW replay set exceeds MAX_BACKTEST_BARS — counted AFTER date-range filtering (bars.retain), so the cap is consistent with source-superset tolerance (a large out-of-window response never fails a valid narrow backtest) and bounds the sort + equity-curve allocation; BacktestEngine::with_max_bars sets a custom cap. Bounding the source's own materialization of the raw response needs the full streaming/bounded-cursor BarSource, deferred to the real Parquet reader (see deferred[]). The engine is co-located with the Internal Simulation Engine because SRS-BT-003 mandates the SAME transaction-cost model family for paper simulation and backtesting (home_rationale). Identical inputs produce an identical BacktestResult — the deterministic-replay seam SRS-BT-010 owns. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-001 passes:false because the launch + data + strategy-execution halves are deferred behind the BarSource / BacktestStrategy ports (see deferred[]): there is no live REST/dashboard launch surface, no real Parquet reader, and no Rust<->Python strategy host yet.
backtest_contract.home_rationale: The SRS module table lists Backtesting and Optimization as a distinct Rust runtime, but SRS-BT-003 mandates the same transaction-cost model family for the internal simulation engine and for backtesting. Co-locating the backtest engine with the InternalSimulationEngine in atp-simulation keeps that shared fill/cost model in one crate; this is the operator-directed home for the slice.

deferred.what: The concrete BarSource implementations — the system catalog reader backed by the unified historical data interface (HistoricalDataAdapter, atp-adapters) and the real arrow/parquet-backed reader for user-uploaded Parquet files (a new external Rust dependency that needs SyRS scope approval; the workspace currently has zero third-party crates) — and the concrete BacktestStrategy that marshals on_bar calls to a Python strategy running under the orchestrator (the Rust<->Python execution boundary, e.g. PyO3 or a subprocess strategy host, also SyRS-gated). The engine models the deterministic replay + integer-money fill loop only; the runtime that supplies real bars and runs real Python strategies flips SRS-BT-001 passes:true.

The live REST handler for POST /api/v1/backtests (declared today as route metadata in python/atp_api/routes.py, which explicitly does not start an HTTP server) and the dashboard backtest controls with start/end date pickers. The acceptance criterion 'start and end dates are selectable through API and dashboard' needs this operator-interface runtime; the engine already accepts a configurable DateRange and echoes it on BacktestResult.range.

BarSource::bars takes a max_bars cap (the engine passes self.max_bars) and its contract requires a source to fail closed with TooManyBars before materializing an oversized Vec; the engine re-checks the in-window count as defense in depth. The FULLY streaming/bounded sorted cursor that never materializes a Vec at all (chunked record-batch iteration) is still deferred to the real arrow/parquet-backed reader, because that shape depends on the library's reader API; the bounded max_bars parameter is the forward-compatible seam, and the Vec port has no concrete implementors yet, so it is not a baked-in external contract. Likewise the fallible strategy port surfaces failures as the single StrategyFailed { reason } today; the typed StrategyTimedOut / StrategyPanicked / marshaling-error taxonomy lands with the concrete Rust<->Python strategy host (same reason-string-now / typed-later pattern as the kill_switch_timeout_contract probe error). Raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-2: the symbol/duplicate/non-positive-price/strategy-failure guards were added in this slice; the streaming-cursor redesign is deferred per the scope decision.

SRS-BT-002 is now IMPLEMENTED on this engine: the configurable transaction-cost model family (commission / slippage / spread-impact, SyRS-default matching) lives in the `cost` module and BacktestEngine::run applies it per fill via the per-run BacktestRequest.cost_config — see the backtest_cost_contract block. BT-001's own engine tests still run frictionless (CostConfig::zero). Still deferred: the operator override SURFACE that lets an operator pick a cost model per run (SRS-BT-002, SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) and the internal simulation engine sharing the SAME family for paper fills (SRS-BT-003).

The full performance metric set (Sharpe, Sortino, alpha, beta, maximum drawdown, annualized return/volatility, win rate). The engine emits the raw trade log + equity curve those metrics are computed from; the metric computations are SRS-BT-004's concern.

Persisted, queryable backtest results (parameters, metrics, trade log, equity curve, benchmark comparison, strategy code version, timestamp). BacktestResult is the in-memory shape; the durable store + query API are SRS-BT-009.

The end-to-end deterministic-backtest guarantee under the real Python strategy host (two identical runs produce identical trade logs, equity curves, and metrics). This slice demonstrates determinism for the Rust engine (stable sort_by_key replay + integer money math); the guarantee across the full Python execution path is SRS-BT-010.

backtest_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-002 (apply configurable commission, slippage, and spread-impact models to backtests). The configurable transaction-cost model FAMILY lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `cost`) and is APPLIED by the runnable backtest engine (module `backtest`). CostConfig bundles three configurable models: CommissionModel (default IbTiered = the published Interactive Brokers tiered schedule — IB_TIERED_RATE_CENTIMINOR_PER_SHARE per share, floored at IB_TIERED_MIN_PER_ORDER_MINOR per order, capped at IB_TIERED_MAX_PCT_BPS of trade value, SYS-15a), SlippageModel (default NotionalBps at DEFAULT_SLIPPAGE_BPS = 0.05% of trade notional, SYS-15b), and SpreadImpactModel (default ObservedOrFallbackBps = half the observed bid-ask spread per share when the bar carries one, else DEFAULT_SPREAD_FALLBACK_BPS = 0.10% of notional, SYS-15c). CostConfig::default() is the SyRS baseline encoded as named constants, so the default is PROVABLY the published value; an operator overrides any model for an individual run (SYS-15d) by building a different CostConfig on BacktestRequest.cost_config — no strategy code changes. All cost math is integer minor units (i128 intermediates, deterministic round-half-up division via div_round_half_up, i64::try_from narrowing -> CostError::Overflow; never f64); every cost is non-negative and the engine always SUBTRACTS it from cash via checked_sub, so a cost can never fabricate cash. The engine fails closed before any fill on a negative configured parameter (CostConfig::validate -> CostError::NegativeParameter) and on a corrupt negative observed bid-ask spread on ANY bar (BacktestError::NegativeSpread, mirroring the BT-001 non-positive-price guard). cost.rs is the SAME transaction-cost model family the internal simulation engine shares for paper fills (SRS-BT-003 deferred half — see deferred[]). The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-002 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-002 passes:false because the operator override SURFACE (the REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator pick a cost model per run, SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) and the real data + Python strategy runtime (SRS-BT-001-runtime) are still deferred.

shared_family.note: SRS-BT-003 shares the cost::CostConfig family for internal simulation paper fills.

deferred.what: The REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator select or override the commission, slippage, and spread-impact models for an individual backtest run (SYS-15d). The engine already accepts a per-run CostConfig on BacktestRequest and applies it to fills; the operator-facing surface that constructs that override from a form / CLI flag / API payload is the deferred half, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-002 passes:false.

IMPLEMENTED in SESSION 39 (SRS-BT-003): the internal simulation engine now consumes this exact cost::CostConfig family for paper fills via the `sim` module -- PaperSimulationEngine::simulate_fill calls the IDENTICAL CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point the backtest engine calls, and defaults to the same SyRS baseline (SYS-15e). See `sim_cost_contract`. The remaining SRS-BT-003 deferrals (SYS-83 limit/stop/fill-probability/volume-cap fill models, live-market-data fills, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence, and the operator per-strategy override surface) live in sim_cost_contract.deferred[].

IbTiered models the published base (<=300k shares/month) per-share rate, the per-order minimum, and the 1%-of-trade-value cap. The cumulative monthly-volume tier ladder (cheaper per-share rates at higher monthly volume) needs cross-run state and is deferred; the per-run rate/floor/cap is the SyRS default baseline.

Same deferral as SRS-BT-001: the real Parquet / system-catalog BarSource and the Rust<->Python strategy host. The cost models are applied by the runnable engine over the in-memory port fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime.

sim_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-003 (use the same transaction-cost model family for internal simulation and backtesting unless configured otherwise; SyRS SYS-15e / SYS-83d; StRS SN-1.03 / SN-1.29). The internal simulation engine applies the SAME transaction-cost model FAMILY (crates/atp-simulation module `cost`, SRS-BT-002) the runnable backtest engine applies, via the new module `sim` paper-fill path. PaperSimulationEngine holds a `cost_config: CostConfig` that derives Default = CostConfig::default(), the IDENTICAL SyRS baseline the backtest engine defaults to (SYS-15e 'same default ... unless explicitly configured otherwise'); an operator overrides it per strategy via with_cost_config without touching strategy code. simulate_fill computes the per-fill CostBreakdown by calling self.cost_config.cost_breakdown(quantity, price_minor, observed_spread_minor) -- the IDENTICAL entry point BacktestEngine::run calls -- and folds the total into a signed cash_delta_minor that ALWAYS SUBTRACTS the cost (a buy pays notional plus cost; a sell receives proceeds less cost), so a cost can never fabricate cash. All money math is integer minor units (i128::from intermediates, i64::try_from -> SimError::Overflow, checked_*; never f64). The fill fails closed before computing any cost on an empty symbol (SimError::EmptySymbol), a non-positive price (SimError::NonPositivePrice), a negative observed spread (SimError::NegativeSpread), and a misconfigured negative cost parameter (SimError::Cost(CostError::NegativeParameter)). The minimal PaperLedger accumulates cash, position, and commission_paid (the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003). The Rust integration test srs_bt_003_shared_cost_family asserts the paper fill's commission / slippage / spread-impact EQUAL the backtest fill's for identical config and inputs (the SRS-BT-003 acceptance criterion). The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-003 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-003 passes:false because the SYS-83 limit/stop/fill-probability/volume-cap fill models, live-market-data fills, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence (SYS-89), and the operator per-strategy override surface (SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) plus the Python strategy runtime are still deferred.

shared_family.note: lib.rs re-exports both `pub mod cost;` and `pub mod sim;`; the internal simulation engine and the backtest engine apply the SAME cost::CostConfig family through the identical CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point (SRS-BT-003 / SYS-15e).

deferred.what: The SYS-83 market/limit/stop/stop-limit fill models, the configurable fill-probability model, the bar-volume fill cap, and fills driven by live bid/ask/last from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70). This slice computes the shared cost decomposition over explicit (quantity, price, spread) fill inputs; the order-type fill logic, volume constraints, and live-data fill source are SRS-SIM-002's half.

The full SYS-84 virtual position ledger -- per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L marked to live data, independent per strategy. PaperLedger here is a minimal cash + position + commission-paid accumulator that proves the shared family's commissions actually accumulate; the full ledger is SRS-SIM-003.
Persisting and restoring the paper ledger, pending simulated orders, and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

The REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator select or override the per-strategy cost model. The engine already accepts a per-strategy CostConfig via PaperSimulationEngine::with_cost_config; the operator-facing surface that constructs that override is the deferred half.

Same deferral as SRS-BT-001/002: the Python strategy host that drives paper fills end to end. The shared cost family is exercised over in-memory fill fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-003 passes:false.
